picks.narrative: IBP values WSH 11.5pp higher than the market — 52.7% model vs 40.4% implied. There's no standout starter, lineup, or bullpen signal driving this — the gap comes from the model's baseline read of the side, not a visible matchup edge. The main pushback is pitcher quality (xfip) (~-0.7pp): Carson Palmquist 4.20 xFIP vs Ryan Weathers 3.51 xFIP. The price has held roughly steady since posting — the current +143 still sits inside the playable range (to +106). The read here is the price, not the profile — the model just makes this a better side than the posted number does.

picks.narrative: IBP makes COL 45.5% fair versus a 39.5% no-vig market price — a 5.5pp gap. No individual baseball factor clears the bar for a headline here — this is not a single visible mismatch, it is the model pricing the side differently than the market. The main pushback is home field (~-0.8pp): SF's home-field context weighs against the pick playing on the road. The price has held roughly steady since posting — the current +150 still sits inside the playable range (to +141). This isn't a clean all-signal agreement — it's the model calling the posted number too generous for the true win probability.

picks.narrative: Model and market disagree on CHC by 5.2pp — 56.7% fair against a 51.5% price. The largest lift is defense (oaa) (~+0.6pp): The model's defensive-efficiency adjustment favors the pick. The market has drifted against the pick since posting, widening the available price — the current -106 still sits inside the playable range (to -111). The edge is the model pricing CHC differently than the market rather than one of the marquee starter/lineup/bullpen buckets — worth taking at the number, not chasing past it.

picks.narrative: The model prices STL at 44.9% to win where the no-vig market implies 40.0%, a 4.9pp disagreement. The model isn't leaning on a marquee matchup here — no single baseball factor rises to a headline; the disagreement is with the posted price itself. The main pushback is pitcher quality (xfip) (~-1.0pp): Kyle Leahy 4.40 xFIP vs Chris Sale 3.31 xFIP. With a headwind that size, this is not a clean all-signal read. The price has held roughly steady since posting — the current +150 still sits inside the playable range (to +144). This is mostly a price play — the model likes the number more than the matchup, so the baseball case is mixed.
